title: Windows Administration: Editions, Settings vs Control Panel, Task Manager & Admin Tools
summary: A deep tour of Windows editions and their feature differences, the Settings/Control Panel divide, Task Manager, the MMC snap-ins and system tools an administrator uses to manage a Windows workstation.
content:
Windows is the dominant client operating system in US government and embassy environments, so an administrator must know how the editions differ and which native tool solves which problem. The four editions tested at A+ Core 2 level are Home, Pro, Pro for Workstations, and Enterprise. The single most important practical difference is that Windows Home cannot join an Active Directory domain — it supports only Workgroup or Azure AD (Entra) personal sign-in — while Pro, Pro for Workstations, and Enterprise can join a domain. Domain membership is what lets a workstation receive centrally managed policy and credentials, so a Home machine is essentially unmanageable in an enterprise and is not deployed in a controlled federal environment.

The edition feature matrix is a frequent exam target. BitLocker full-disk encryption is available on Pro, Pro for Workstations, and Enterprise, but NOT on Home (Home offers only the weaker 'Device Encryption' on supported hardware). The local Group Policy Editor (gpedit.msc) and the ability to receive domain Group Policy are Pro-and-up. Remote Desktop as a HOST (accepting incoming RDP connections) requires Pro or higher — Home can only act as an RDP client. Other Pro-and-up features include Hyper-V, Assigned Access/kiosk mode, and Windows Sandbox. Enterprise adds volume-licensing-only capabilities such as DirectAccess, AppLocker, BranchCache, and Windows To Go (legacy). Pro for Workstations targets high-end hardware with ReFS, persistent memory, faster file sharing (SMB Direct), and support for up to 4 CPUs.

• RAM limits are a classic memorization item. 32-bit Windows is capped at 4 GB of addressable RAM regardless of edition. For 64-bit Windows: Home supports up to 128 GB; Pro up to 2 TB; Pro for Workstations and Enterprise up to 6 TB. Knowing 'Home = 128 GB, Pro = 2 TB' is usually enough.

Modern Windows splits configuration between the newer Settings app and the legacy Control Panel. Microsoft is steadily migrating functions into Settings, but many deep applets still live in Control Panel, so an administrator must be fluent in both. Settings (the touch-friendly app) covers Windows Update, accounts, network basics, and personalization. Control Panel applets you must recognize include: System (view OS version, computer name, environment variables, and access System Protection/Remote settings), Network and Sharing Center (adapters, status, sharing), Devices and Printers, Programs and Features (uninstall/repair software and 'Turn Windows features on or off'), User Accounts, Windows Defender Firewall (with Advanced Security for inbound/outbound rules), Power Options (Balanced/Power saver/High performance plans, and what the power button does), Credential Manager (the vault storing saved Windows and web credentials), and Device Manager (manage hardware and drivers).

Task Manager (Ctrl+Shift+Esc) is the first-line tool for performance and process triage, and you should know every tab. The Processes tab shows running apps and background processes with live CPU, memory, disk, network, and GPU usage and lets you End Task on a hung program. The Performance tab graphs CPU, memory, disk, Ethernet/Wi-Fi, and GPU utilization over time and exposes details like uptime and the link to Resource Monitor. The App history tab tracks resource use by Microsoft Store apps. The Startup (Startup apps) tab enables/disables programs that launch at boot and shows their startup impact — the modern replacement for msconfig's old Startup tab. The Users tab shows logged-on users and their resource consumption (useful on RDP/terminal hosts). The Details tab is the advanced process view with PIDs, status, and the ability to set priority and CPU affinity. The Services tab lists Windows services with their state and lets you start/stop them or jump to services.msc.

The Microsoft Management Console (MMC) hosts 'snap-ins' — the .msc consoles that do the heavy administrative lifting. Memorize these by file name: services.msc (Services — manage service startup type and state), devmgmt.msc (Device Manager — drivers and hardware), diskmgmt.msc (Disk Management — partitions, volumes, drive letters, format, extend/shrink), gpedit.msc (Local Group Policy Editor — Pro+ only), eventvwr.msc (Event Viewer — Application/Security/System logs and custom views), regedit (Registry Editor — the hierarchical config database with hives like HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE and HKEY_CURRENT_USER), perfmon.msc (Performance Monitor — counters and Data Collector Sets), taskschd.msc (Task Scheduler — run programs on triggers/schedules), and lusrmgr.msc (Local Users and Groups — note this snap-in is NOT present on Home editions).

• Disk Management (diskmgmt.msc) is where you initialize new disks (choosing GPT or MBR), create/delete/format partitions, assign or change drive letters, mark a partition active, and extend or shrink volumes — all without a reboot for most operations.

User Account Control (UAC) is the security feature that prevents silent privilege escalation. Even an administrator runs with a standard-user token by default; when an action requires elevation, UAC dims the desktop (Secure Desktop) and prompts for consent (admin) or credentials (standard user). The slider in Control Panel sets how aggressively UAC prompts. UAC is why you 'Run as administrator' for tools like an elevated command prompt — it does not change WHO you are, it requests use of your full administrator token for that task. Disabling UAC is a security finding in any hardened federal build.

Finally, a cluster of standalone system tools rounds out the toolkit. msconfig (System Configuration) controls boot options (Safe Boot, boot logging, number of processors) and service startup, and is used for clean-boot troubleshooting; note that startup PROGRAM management moved to Task Manager. msinfo32 (System Information) is the read-only inventory of hardware, components, and the software environment — the go-to for spec-gathering. dxdiag (DirectX Diagnostic Tool) reports display, sound, and DirectX details and is used to diagnose graphics/audio issues. resmon (Resource Monitor) is the detailed real-time view of CPU, memory, disk, and network at the per-process and per-handle level — far deeper than Task Manager's Performance tab. Together these tools let an administrator inventory, monitor, configure, and troubleshoot a Windows workstation entirely with built-in utilities.
keyPoints: Windows Home cannot join an Active Directory domain (Workgroup/Entra only); Pro, Pro for Workstations, and Enterprise can.
BitLocker, gpedit.msc, and acting as an RDP host all require Pro or higher — Home lacks them (Home has only 'Device Encryption' and is RDP-client only).
64-bit RAM limits: Home 128 GB, Pro 2 TB, Pro for Workstations & Enterprise 6 TB; all 32-bit Windows caps at 4 GB.
Enterprise adds volume-license features (AppLocker, BranchCache, DirectAccess); Pro for Workstations adds ReFS, persistent memory, and up to 4 CPUs.
Settings is the modern app (Update, accounts, network); Control Panel still hosts deep applets like System, Firewall, Power Options, Credential Manager, and Device Manager.
Task Manager tabs: Processes, Performance, App history, Startup, Users, Details, Services; Startup-program management lives here, not in msconfig anymore.
Key .msc snap-ins: services.msc, devmgmt.msc, diskmgmt.msc, gpedit.msc, eventvwr.msc, perfmon.msc, taskschd.msc, lusrmgr.msc; plus regedit.
Disk Management (diskmgmt.msc) initializes disks (GPT/MBR), creates/formats partitions, assigns drive letters, and extends/shrinks volumes.
UAC keeps admins on a standard-user token and prompts (on the Secure Desktop) before elevating; disabling it is a hardening violation.
System tools: msconfig (boot/clean-boot), msinfo32 (read-only inventory), dxdiag (graphics/audio/DirectX), resmon (deep real-time resource view).

flashcards.front: Which Windows editions can join an Active Directory domain?
flashcards.back: Pro, Pro for Workstations, and Enterprise. Home cannot — it supports only Workgroup or personal Entra/Microsoft account sign-in.

flashcards.front: What full-disk encryption feature is missing from Windows Home?
flashcards.back: BitLocker. Home offers only the weaker 'Device Encryption' on supported hardware; BitLocker requires Pro or higher.

flashcards.front: What edition is required to act as a Remote Desktop (RDP) host?
flashcards.back: Pro or higher. Windows Home can only be an RDP client, not accept incoming connections.

flashcards.front: State the 64-bit RAM limits for Home and Pro.
flashcards.back: Home supports up to 128 GB; Pro supports up to 2 TB (Pro for Workstations and Enterprise go to 6 TB).

flashcards.front: What is the maximum RAM any 32-bit edition of Windows can address?
flashcards.back: 4 GB, regardless of edition.

flashcards.front: Which tool opens the Local Group Policy Editor, and on which editions?
flashcards.back: gpedit.msc — available only on Pro, Pro for Workstations, and Enterprise (not Home).

flashcards.front: List the tabs of Task Manager.
flashcards.back: Processes, Performance, App history, Startup (Startup apps), Users, Details, and Services.

flashcards.front: Where do you now enable/disable startup programs (formerly the msconfig Startup tab)?
flashcards.back: The Startup (Startup apps) tab of Task Manager.

flashcards.front: What does services.msc manage?
flashcards.back: Windows services — their state (start/stop) and startup type (Automatic, Manual, Disabled).

flashcards.front: What does diskmgmt.msc let you do?
flashcards.back: Initialize disks (GPT/MBR), create/delete/format partitions, assign drive letters, mark active, and extend/shrink volumes.

flashcards.front: What is the file name for Device Manager? For Event Viewer?
flashcards.back: devmgmt.msc for Device Manager; eventvwr.msc for Event Viewer.

flashcards.front: What is the function of taskschd.msc?
flashcards.back: Task Scheduler — runs programs or scripts automatically based on time schedules or event triggers.

flashcards.front: Which snap-in manages local users and groups, and where is it unavailable?
flashcards.back: lusrmgr.msc (Local Users and Groups); it is not present on Windows Home editions.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of Credential Manager?
flashcards.back: It is the Windows vault that stores saved Windows and web credentials for automatic sign-in.

flashcards.front: What does User Account Control (UAC) do?
flashcards.back: It keeps even administrators on a standard-user token by default and prompts on the Secure Desktop for consent/credentials before elevating a task.

flashcards.front: What does msconfig (System Configuration) control?
flashcards.back: Boot options (Safe Boot, boot logging, processor count) and service startup; used for clean-boot troubleshooting.

flashcards.front: Contrast msinfo32 and resmon.
flashcards.back: msinfo32 is a read-only inventory of hardware and the software environment; resmon (Resource Monitor) is a deep real-time view of CPU, memory, disk, and network per process.

flashcards.front: What does dxdiag report?
flashcards.back: DirectX diagnostics — display, sound, and input device details — used to troubleshoot graphics and audio.

flashcards.front: Where in Control Panel do you create inbound/outbound rules?
flashcards.back: Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security.

flashcards.front: What does regedit edit, and name two root hives.
flashcards.back: The Windows Registry (the hierarchical configuration database); root hives include HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE (HKLM) and HKEY_CURRENT_USER (HKCU).

title: Command Line, File Systems & Permissions: CMD, PowerShell, Bash, and NTFS/Share Security
summary: A deep reference on Windows CMD and PowerShell, essential Linux/bash commands, file system characteristics and limits, and how NTFS and share permissions combine.
content:
Command-line fluency is essential because automation, remote administration, and troubleshooting all depend on it, and the DTOT covers both Windows and Linux shells. Start with the Windows Command Prompt (CMD). Networking commands are heavily tested: ipconfig displays IP configuration (ipconfig /all shows MAC, DNS, and DHCP details; /release and /renew cycle a DHCP lease; /flushdns clears the DNS resolver cache). ping tests reachability and latency via ICMP echo. tracert traces the router hops to a destination. netstat shows active connections and listening ports (netstat -an for numeric listing of all). nslookup queries DNS to resolve names to addresses and vice versa.

System and disk maintenance commands: sfc /scannow (System File Checker) scans and repairs protected Windows system files. chkdsk checks a disk for errors (chkdsk /f fixes file-system errors; /r locates bad sectors and recovers readable data). diskpart is the scriptable command-line partition manager (list disk, select disk, clean, create partition, format). gpupdate forces a Group Policy refresh (gpupdate /force reapplies all policy), and gpresult /r shows the Resultant Set of Policy applied to the user/computer. shutdown controls power state (shutdown /s = shutdown, /r = restart, /t sets a delay, /a aborts a pending shutdown). winver shows the Windows version and build in a dialog; hostname prints the computer name.

• File and directory commands: dir lists directory contents; cd changes directory; md (mkdir) and rd (rmdir) make and remove directories; copy copies files; xcopy copies files and directory trees (older); robocopy ('Robust File Copy') is the modern, resilient copier that mirrors directories, retries on failure, and preserves attributes (robocopy SRC DST /MIR mirrors a tree, /E copies subdirectories including empty ones). format prepares a volume with a file system. tasklist lists running processes (with PIDs); taskkill ends them (taskkill /PID nnnn /F force-kills, or /IM name.exe by image name).

PowerShell is the modern, object-oriented shell and scripting language, and it largely supersedes CMD for administration. Its commands are cmdlets in a consistent Verb-Noun form (Get-Process, Stop-Service, Set-Location, Get-ChildItem). Unlike CMD, PowerShell passes .NET objects down the pipeline rather than plain text, so you can filter and act on properties directly. Get-Help <cmdlet> retrieves documentation (Get-Help Get-Process -Examples). Scripts are saved as .ps1 files. A key security concept is the Execution Policy, which governs whether scripts may run: Restricted (default on clients — no scripts), RemoteSigned (local scripts run; downloaded scripts must be signed), AllSigned (all scripts must be signed), and Unrestricted/Bypass (least safe). Set it with Set-ExecutionPolicy; it is a safety guardrail, not a true security boundary.

Linux/bash essentials are equally testable because servers, network appliances, and embassy infrastructure often run Linux. Navigation and files: ls lists files (ls -la for long listing including hidden files), cd changes directory, pwd prints the working directory, cp copies, mv moves/renames, rm removes (rm -r recursively; rm -rf forcibly — dangerous), cat displays/concatenates file contents, grep searches text by pattern (grep -i for case-insensitive). Permissions: chmod changes permission bits (e.g., chmod 755 = rwx for owner, r-x for group/others; chmod +x adds execute), chown changes file owner (and group). sudo runs a single command with elevated (root) privileges — the Linux analog to 'Run as administrator'. Package management is distro-specific: apt (apt-get) for Debian/Ubuntu, yum or dnf for Red Hat/Fedora/CentOS. man <command> opens the manual page. Process and system: ps lists processes (ps aux shows all), top shows live process/resource usage. Networking: ip addr (modern) or ifconfig (legacy) shows interface configuration. Text editors at the terminal: nano (beginner-friendly) and vi/vim (powerful, modal).

File systems determine how data is stored and what limits apply, and the differences are exam staples. FAT32 is old and universally compatible but has a 4 GB maximum FILE size and a 2 TB volume limit, and it is not journaled — making it suitable mainly for small USB drives and firmware/EFI partitions. exFAT was designed for flash media and removes FAT32's size limits (huge files and volumes), with broad cross-platform support, but it is also not journaled. NTFS is the native Windows file system: it is journaled (resilient to crashes), supports file/folder permissions (ACLs), encryption (EFS), compression, quotas, shadow copies, and very large files/volumes — it is the standard for Windows system and data drives. ext4 is the common Linux journaling file system (large files/volumes, robust). APFS (Apple File System) is macOS's modern file system, optimized for SSDs with snapshots, cloning, and strong encryption (replacing the older HFS+).

• A critical interoperability point: a single USB drive formatted FAT32 can be read AND written by Windows, macOS, and Linux, which is why it (or exFAT for large files) is chosen for cross-platform transfer; NTFS is natively read/write on Windows but often read-only on macOS without extra software.

Windows access control hinges on two distinct permission sets that beginners conflate. NTFS permissions (security permissions) apply to files and folders on an NTFS volume and govern access for both local and network users; the standard levels are Full Control, Modify, Read & Execute, List Folder Contents, Read, and Write. Share permissions apply ONLY when a folder is accessed over the network through a share, and have just three levels: Full Control, Change, and Read. The two combine by a strict rule: when a user reaches a resource over the network, the effective permission is the MORE RESTRICTIVE (the intersection) of the share permission and the NTFS permission. So if Share = Read but NTFS = Full Control, a network user gets Read; if Share = Full Control but NTFS = Read, the user still gets Read. Accessing the same folder locally bypasses share permissions entirely — only NTFS applies. A common best practice is to set Share to 'Authenticated Users: Full Control' and then control everything precisely with NTFS permissions.

• Inheritance: by default, child files and folders inherit the NTFS permissions of their parent. You can break inheritance to set explicit permissions, and explicit Deny generally overrides Allow. Ownership: every object has an owner who can always change its permissions; an administrator can 'Take Ownership' to regain control of a file. Attributes: files carry flags such as Read-only (R), Hidden (H), System (S), and Archive (A), set via the attrib command or the file's Properties — these are separate from, and weaker than, NTFS permissions.
keyPoints: Core CMD networking: ipconfig (/all, /flushdns, /release, /renew), ping, tracert, netstat -an, nslookup.
CMD maintenance: sfc /scannow repairs system files; chkdsk /f fixes errors and /r recovers bad sectors; diskpart scripts partitions; gpupdate /force and gpresult /r handle Group Policy.
robocopy is the resilient modern copier (/MIR mirrors, /E includes empty subfolders); taskkill /PID n /F or /IM name.exe ends processes from tasklist.
PowerShell uses Verb-Noun cmdlets and passes objects (not text); Get-Help documents cmdlets; scripts are .ps1 files.
PowerShell Execution Policy: Restricted (default), RemoteSigned, AllSigned, Unrestricted/Bypass — a safety guardrail set with Set-ExecutionPolicy, not a real security boundary.
Linux essentials: ls/cd/pwd/cp/mv/rm/cat/grep; chmod (e.g., 755) and chown for permissions; sudo for root; apt vs yum/dnf; man, ps/top, ip/ifconfig, nano/vi.
FAT32 has a 4 GB max file size (≈2 TB volume) and no journaling; exFAT removes those limits for flash but is also unjournaled.
NTFS is the journaled Windows file system with ACL permissions, EFS encryption, compression, and quotas; ext4 is Linux's journaling FS; APFS is macOS's modern FS.
NTFS permissions (Full Control/Modify/Read & Execute/List/Read/Write) apply locally and over the network; share permissions (Full Control/Change/Read) apply only over the network.
When combined over the network, the effective access is the MOST RESTRICTIVE of share and NTFS; local access ignores share permissions.
NTFS permissions inherit from parent to child by default; inheritance can be broken, and explicit Deny overrides Allow.
Every object has an owner who can reset its permissions; admins can Take Ownership. File attributes (R/H/S/A) are set via attrib and are separate from NTFS permissions.

flashcards.front: What does ipconfig /flushdns do?
flashcards.back: Clears the local DNS resolver cache, forcing fresh name lookups.

flashcards.front: Difference between chkdsk /f and chkdsk /r?
flashcards.back: /f fixes file-system errors; /r also locates bad sectors and recovers readable data (implies /f).

flashcards.front: What does sfc /scannow do?
flashcards.back: System File Checker scans protected Windows system files and repairs corrupted or missing ones.

flashcards.front: Which command forces a Group Policy refresh, and which shows applied policy?
flashcards.back: gpupdate /force forces a refresh; gpresult /r shows the Resultant Set of Policy.

flashcards.front: What is robocopy and what does /MIR do?
flashcards.back: Robust File Copy — a resilient directory copier; /MIR mirrors a directory tree (copies and deletes to make destination match source).

flashcards.front: How do you force-kill a process by its PID at the command line?
flashcards.back: taskkill /PID <number> /F (or /IM <image.exe> /F by name).

flashcards.front: What form do all PowerShell cmdlets take, and what flows through the pipeline?
flashcards.back: Verb-Noun (e.g., Get-Process); PowerShell passes .NET objects, not plain text.

flashcards.front: What is the default PowerShell execution policy on clients, and what does RemoteSigned do?
flashcards.back: Default is Restricted (no scripts). RemoteSigned allows local scripts to run but requires downloaded scripts to be digitally signed.

flashcards.front: What does chmod 755 grant?
flashcards.back: rwx (read/write/execute) for the owner, and r-x (read/execute) for group and others.

flashcards.front: What is the Linux equivalent of 'Run as administrator'?
flashcards.back: sudo — runs a single command with elevated (root) privileges.

flashcards.front: Which package managers go with which Linux families?
flashcards.back: apt/apt-get for Debian/Ubuntu; yum or dnf for Red Hat/Fedora/CentOS.

flashcards.front: Name the modern and legacy Linux commands to view interface IP configuration.
flashcards.back: ip addr (modern) and ifconfig (legacy).

flashcards.front: What is FAT32's maximum individual file size?
flashcards.back: 4 GB (one byte under 4 GiB); it is also unjournaled.

flashcards.front: Why choose exFAT over FAT32 for a flash drive?
flashcards.back: exFAT removes FAT32's 4 GB file-size and 2 TB volume limits while keeping broad cross-platform compatibility.

flashcards.front: Which features distinguish NTFS from FAT/exFAT?
flashcards.back: Journaling, file/folder ACL permissions, EFS encryption, compression, quotas, and shadow copies.

flashcards.front: What is the native modern file system of macOS, and of Linux?
flashcards.back: APFS for macOS; ext4 is the common Linux journaling file system.

flashcards.front: List the three share-permission levels.
flashcards.back: Full Control, Change, and Read.

flashcards.front: How do NTFS and share permissions combine for a network user?
flashcards.back: The effective permission is the MOST RESTRICTIVE (the intersection) of the share permission and the NTFS permission.

flashcards.front: If Share = Full Control but NTFS = Read, what does a network user get?
flashcards.back: Read — the most restrictive of the two wins.

flashcards.front: Do share permissions affect a user logged in locally at the machine?
flashcards.back: No. Share permissions apply only over the network; local access is governed solely by NTFS permissions.

flashcards.front: What happens to NTFS permissions by default on new files in a folder?
flashcards.back: They inherit the parent folder's permissions; inheritance can be broken to set explicit permissions, and explicit Deny overrides Allow.

flashcards.front: What can the owner of a file always do, and how can an admin regain control?
flashcards.back: The owner can always change the file's permissions; an administrator can use 'Take Ownership' to become the owner.

title: Cross-Platform OS, Installation, Boot & Lifecycle: macOS, Linux, Deployment, UEFI/GPT, and Patching
summary: A deep guide to macOS and Linux features, OS installation and deployment methods, boot firmware and partition schemes, virtualization, and the OS lifecycle including updates, drivers, and end-of-life.
content:
Embassy IT supports a mix of Windows, macOS, and Linux, so a Diplomatic Technology Officer must understand each platform's signature features and the mechanics of installing, booting, and maintaining operating systems. macOS ships with a set of named utilities you must recognize. Time Machine is the built-in incremental backup system that automatically saves versioned snapshots to an external or network disk, allowing point-in-time restores. Finder is the macOS file manager (the analog of Windows File Explorer). Spotlight is the system-wide search (and quick launcher), invoked with Command+Space. Disk Utility manages disks and volumes — formatting, partitioning, creating disk images, and running First Aid to repair file-system errors (the rough equivalent of Disk Management plus chkdsk). Keychain (Keychain Access) is the macOS credential vault that stores passwords, keys, and certificates — the analog of Windows Credential Manager. Gatekeeper enforces that only signed/notarized or trusted apps run, protecting against unsigned malware. FileVault is macOS full-disk encryption (the analog of BitLocker). Mission Control gives an overview of all open windows, desktops (Spaces), and full-screen apps for quick switching.

Linux is distributed as 'distributions' (distros) built on the Linux kernel, differing in package management, defaults, and support model. Debian and Ubuntu use the APT package manager and .deb packages; Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL), Fedora, and CentOS/Rocky/Alma use RPM packages with yum or the newer dnf. Other notable distros include SUSE (zypper) and Arch (pacman). For government and embassy server use, long-term-support (LTS) and enterprise distros like Ubuntu LTS and RHEL are preferred for their multi-year patch commitments. A package manager resolves dependencies, installs/updates/removes software, and pulls from curated repositories — a major reliability and security advantage over downloading installers manually.

Installing or deploying an OS can be done several ways, and matching the method to the situation is a common exam theme. A clean install wipes the target and installs fresh — the most reliable result and the standard for a known-good baseline, but it erases existing data and apps. An in-place upgrade installs a newer OS over the existing one, keeping apps, settings, and files — convenient but it can carry forward problems. Image deployment / cloning captures a pre-configured 'golden image' (with OS, patches, and apps) and applies it to many machines for fast, consistent provisioning — the standard for fleets; tools include Microsoft tools (DISM/Sysprep, MDT, SCCM/Configuration Manager, Autopilot) and third-party imagers. PXE / network boot lets a machine boot from the network and pull an installer or image from a server — ideal for mass deployment with no media. A recovery / repair install reinstalls or resets the OS while attempting to preserve data, used to fix a broken system (Windows 'Reset this PC' / recovery environment, macOS Recovery). An unattended (answer-file) installation automates the install with a script (Windows unattend.xml; Linux Kickstart/preseed) so no manual input is needed.

Boot methods and firmware are foundational. Firmware comes in two flavors: legacy BIOS (older, 16-bit, limited to MBR disks) and UEFI (modern firmware with a graphical setup, faster boot, support for very large disks, and security features like Secure Boot, which only allows signed bootloaders to load — a defense against bootkits). UEFI pairs with the GPT partition scheme; BIOS pairs with MBR. The partition-scheme distinction is heavily tested: MBR (Master Boot Record) supports a maximum of 4 primary partitions (or 3 primary + 1 extended holding logical drives) and a maximum disk size of 2 TB. GPT (GUID Partition Table) supports up to 128 partitions (in Windows) and disks far larger than 2 TB, and it stores backup copies of the partition table for resilience. GPT requires UEFI to boot Windows. You can boot/install from several media by selecting the boot device or order in firmware (or a one-time boot menu): boot from USB (the most common today for installers), from optical (CD/DVD, legacy), or from the network (PXE).

• During installation you also handle partitioning and formatting: you create one or more partitions on the target disk and format them with a file system (NTFS for Windows system volumes; ext4 commonly for Linux; APFS for macOS). On a UEFI/GPT Windows install the setup also creates supporting partitions — notably the EFI System Partition (formatted FAT32) and a Microsoft Reserved/recovery partition.

Virtualization is invaluable for safely testing and running operating systems. A hypervisor creates virtual machines (VMs) — isolated, software-defined computers each with their own virtual disk and OS. Type 1 (bare-metal) hypervisors run directly on hardware (Hyper-V, VMware ESXi, KVM) and are used for servers; Type 2 (hosted) hypervisors run as an app on top of an existing OS (VirtualBox, VMware Workstation/Player, Parallels on Mac) and are ideal for a technician testing an OS, trying patches, or running a different platform on a laptop. VMs use snapshots to capture state before a risky change and roll back instantly, which makes them perfect sandboxes for evaluating an unfamiliar or potentially malicious OS image. Adequate host CPU, RAM, and (for Type 1/efficient Type 2) hardware virtualization extensions (Intel VT-x / AMD-V) enabled in firmware are prerequisites.

Finally, every OS has a lifecycle that drives patching, driver, and end-of-life (EOL) decisions — a security-critical topic in government. Vendors release regular updates: security patches (fix vulnerabilities — highest priority), bug fixes, and feature updates. Windows distributes these via Windows Update and, in enterprises, through managed channels (WSUS / Configuration Manager / Intune) so administrators can test and stage them; Windows quality updates arrive monthly ('Patch Tuesday'), with larger feature updates periodically. Drivers — the software that lets the OS talk to hardware — must be kept current for stability and security and are obtained from the OS update service or the hardware vendor; Device Manager handles install/update/rollback. The end-of-life or end-of-support date is when a vendor stops issuing security updates; running an EOL OS (e.g., Windows 7, or Windows 10 after its October 14, 2025 end of support) is a serious risk because new vulnerabilities go unpatched, and it typically violates federal security policy. Organizations therefore plan timely migrations, may purchase Extended Security Updates (ESU) as a stopgap, and inventory systems to ensure nothing in production is past EOL.
keyPoints: macOS utilities: Time Machine (backup), Finder (file manager), Spotlight (search), Disk Utility (disks/First Aid), Keychain (credential vault), Gatekeeper (app-signing enforcement), FileVault (full-disk encryption), Mission Control (window/desktop overview).
macOS analogs: Keychain≈Credential Manager, FileVault≈BitLocker, Disk Utility≈Disk Management, Time Machine≈File History/backup, Spotlight≈Windows Search.
Linux distros differ by package manager: Debian/Ubuntu use APT (.deb); RHEL/Fedora/CentOS use RPM via yum/dnf; package managers resolve dependencies from trusted repos.

Install methods: clean install (wipe, baseline), in-place upgrade (keep apps/data), image/clone deployment (golden image to fleets), PXE/network boot, recovery/repair, and unattended (unattend.xml / Kickstart/preseed).
Firmware: legacy BIOS (MBR, limited) vs UEFI (GPT, faster, Secure Boot allowing only signed bootloaders).
MBR supports max 4 primary partitions and a 2 TB disk limit; GPT supports up to 128 partitions, disks far over 2 TB, and backup partition tables; GPT needs UEFI to boot Windows.
Boot media is selected in firmware/boot menu: USB (common), optical (legacy), or network/PXE.
Installation includes partitioning and formatting (NTFS for Windows, ext4 for Linux, APFS for macOS); UEFI Windows creates an EFI System Partition formatted FAT32.

Type 1 (bare-metal) hypervisors run on hardware (Hyper-V, ESXi, KVM) for servers; Type 2 (hosted) run on an OS (VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, Parallels) for testing; snapshots enable instant rollback. VT-x/AMD-V must be enabled.
OS lifecycle: prioritize security patches; Windows updates via Windows Update or managed WSUS/Intune ('Patch Tuesday' monthly); keep drivers current via Device Manager/vendor.

Running an end-of-life OS (no more security updates, e.g., Windows 10 after Oct 14, 2025) is a major risk and usually a federal policy violation; plan migrations or buy Extended Security Updates (ESU) as a stopgap.

flashcards.front: What is macOS Time Machine?
flashcards.back: The built-in incremental backup system that saves versioned snapshots to an external/network disk for point-in-time restore.

flashcards.front: What is the macOS equivalent of Windows BitLocker?
flashcards.back: FileVault — macOS full-disk encryption.

flashcards.front: What does Gatekeeper do on macOS?
flashcards.back: Enforces that only signed/notarized or trusted applications can run, blocking unsigned malware.

flashcards.front: What is the macOS analog of Windows Credential Manager?
flashcards.back: Keychain (Keychain Access) — stores passwords, keys, and certificates.

flashcards.front: Which package managers are used by Debian/Ubuntu vs Red Hat/Fedora?
flashcards.back: APT (.deb) for Debian/Ubuntu; RPM via yum or dnf for Red Hat/Fedora/CentOS.

flashcards.front: Clean install vs in-place upgrade — key difference?
flashcards.back: A clean install wipes the target for a fresh baseline (erasing data); an in-place upgrade installs over the existing OS, keeping apps, settings, and files.

flashcards.front: What is image/clone deployment used for?
flashcards.back: Applying a pre-configured 'golden image' to many machines for fast, consistent provisioning of a fleet.

flashcards.front: What is PXE boot?
flashcards.back: Booting a machine from the network so it can pull an OS installer or image from a server — ideal for mass deployment without media.

flashcards.front: What files automate unattended installs on Windows and Linux?
flashcards.back: Windows uses an answer file (unattend.xml); Linux uses Kickstart (Red Hat) or preseed (Debian/Ubuntu).

flashcards.front: What partition scheme pairs with UEFI, and which with legacy BIOS?
flashcards.back: GPT pairs with UEFI; MBR pairs with legacy BIOS.

flashcards.front: What are MBR's partition and disk-size limits?
flashcards.back: Maximum 4 primary partitions (or 3 primary + 1 extended) and a 2 TB disk size limit.

flashcards.front: What advantages does GPT have over MBR?
flashcards.back: Up to 128 partitions (Windows), support for disks far larger than 2 TB, and a backup copy of the partition table for resilience.

flashcards.front: What is Secure Boot?
flashcards.back: A UEFI feature that allows only digitally signed bootloaders to load, defending against bootkits/rootkits.

flashcards.front: What file system is the EFI System Partition formatted with on a UEFI install?
flashcards.back: FAT32.

flashcards.front: Type 1 vs Type 2 hypervisor?
flashcards.back: Type 1 (bare-metal) runs directly on hardware (Hyper-V, ESXi, KVM) for servers; Type 2 (hosted) runs as an app on an existing OS (VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, Parallels) for testing.

flashcards.front: Why are VM snapshots useful for testing an OS or patch?
flashcards.back: They capture the VM's state before a risky change so you can instantly roll back if something breaks.

flashcards.front: What firmware setting must be enabled to run hardware-accelerated VMs?
flashcards.back: Hardware virtualization extensions — Intel VT-x or AMD-V.

flashcards.front: Why is running an end-of-life (EOL) operating system dangerous?
flashcards.back: The vendor no longer issues security updates, so new vulnerabilities go unpatched; it typically also violates federal security policy.

flashcards.front: What is the role of Device Manager regarding drivers?
flashcards.back: It installs, updates, disables, and rolls back hardware drivers — the software that lets the OS communicate with hardware.

flashcards.front: What is 'Patch Tuesday' and how do enterprises stage updates?
flashcards.back: Microsoft's monthly Windows quality-update release (second Tuesday); enterprises test and stage updates via WSUS, Configuration Manager, or Intune before broad rollout.

flashcards.front: What is an option to keep running an OS briefly past end-of-support more safely?
flashcards.back: Purchasing Extended Security Updates (ESU) as a temporary stopgap while migration is completed.

flashcards.front: Which macOS tool repairs file-system errors, and what is it called?
flashcards.back: Disk Utility, using its First Aid function.
